# Open peer-reviewed Etruscan reference

Retrieved 2026-07-10. This is reference literature, not a corpus and not a set
of gold labels.

## File

### `belfiore_2020_etruscan_overview.pdf`

- Valentina Belfiore, “Etrusco” / “Etruscan”, *Palaeohispanica* 20 (2020),
  pp. 199–262.
- DOI: <https://doi.org/10.36707/palaeohispanica.v0i20.382>
- Article page: <https://www.ifc-ojs.es/index.php/palaeohispanica/article/view/382>
- Download used: <https://ifc-ojs.es/index.php/palaeohispanica/article/download/382/356/2146>
- 64 PDF pages; 1,445,122 bytes as retrieved.
- License: CC BY-NC-SA. The journal states that all journal content is under
  Creative Commons CC BY-NC-SA and is openly accessible.

## Why it is useful

This is a peer-reviewed, field-specific state-of-the-art survey covering
interpretive methods, printed and online corpora, inscription counts and
typology, phonology, morphology, linguistic typology, syntax, lexicon,
writing, and the onomastic system. It estimates that the broadly defined
documentary corpus probably exceeds 11,000 attestations when marks, alphabetic
and numeric graffiti, non-alphabetic signs, and sigla are included. That
definition is much broader than “language-encoding texts”, so the number must
not be used as a training-set size without explicit genre/sign filtering.

Use the paper as an audit checklist and bibliography map. Any lexical or
grammatical claim imported into code or data still needs inscription-level
support and an uncertainty/provenance field.

Integrity hashes are in `SHA256SUMS`. Redistribution must preserve attribution
and the CC BY-NC-SA terms.
